items.source: And you know what they call a Quarter Pounder with cheese in Paris? Got the metric system there. They wouldn't know what the fuck a Quarter Pounder is.
items.reference: ¿Sabes como lle chaman ao Cuarto de Libra con Queso en París? Alí teñen o sistema métrico; non saberían o que é un cuarto de libra.

general.text: Vincent reveals that McDonald's in France sells the Quarter Pounder under a different name (Le Royal with Cheese) because French customers use the metric system and wouldn't understand a weight in pounds. The comedy is Vincent's tone: he treats this as a profound philosophical discovery about cultural difference, not a mundane marketing fact. In Spain and Galicia, the metric system is also standard — the audience understands Vincent's amazement as something distinctly American.
general.nb: The actual Spanish McDonald's name at the time was 'McRoyal con Queso'. A translator may choose to use this, or keep 'Cuarto de Libra' to preserve Vincent's logic (he is explaining why the name changes, so the original name must appear).

glg.nb: In Galician, 'chaman' (they call) is the correct form; avoid the Castilian-influenced 'llaman'. The rest of the construction mirrors Spanish closely but watch for gallicisms introduced by proximity to Castilian in everyday speech.

items.candidate:
E sabes como lle chaman
en París a un Quarter Pounder con queixo?
Non lle chaman
Quarter Pounder con queixo?
Alí teñen o sistema métrico. Non terían
nin idea do que carallo é un Quarter Pounder.

issues.justification: The line is understandable and keeps the joke, but the sentence break and the repeated 'Quarter Pounder con queixo' make it feel a bit clunky compared with the smoother reference.

items.source: The path of the righteous man is beset on all sides by the inequities of the selfish and the tyranny of evil men. Blessed is he who, in the name of charity and good will, shepherds the weak through the valley of darkness, for he is truly his brother's keeper and the finder of lost children. And I will strike down upon thee with great vengeance and furious anger those who attempt to poison and destroy my brothers. And you will know my name is the Lord when I lay my vengeance upon thee!
items.reference: O camiño do home xusto está asediado por todos os lados polas iniquidades do egoísta e a tiranía dos homes malvados. Bendito sexa aquel que, en nome da caridade e a boa vontade, guíe aos débiles polo val das tebras, pois el é verdadeiramente o gardián do seu irmán e o achador dos nenos perdidos. E golpearei sobre vós con grande vinganza e furiosa ira sobre aqueles que intenten envelenar e destruír aos meus irmáns. E saberedes que son o Señor cando descargue a miña vinganza sobre vós.

general.text: This is largely Tarantino's invention, only loosely based on Ezekiel 25:17. The register is deliberately archaic and biblical — 'beset,' 'thee,' 'shepherds the weak through the valley' — creating a violent irony: Jules, a hitman, delivers sacred language as a prelude to murder. The challenge is to maintain this archaic register in translation. By the end of the film, Jules admits he never understood the passage but liked saying it; it has become ritual rather than meaning.
general.nb: The archaic second-person 'thee' is crucial. Spanish 'vosotros' and Galician 'vós' are the closest equivalents — rarely used in Latin American Spanish but natural in Iberian Spanish and Galician liturgical language.

glg.nb: 'Tebras' (darkness/shadows) is the correct Galician form; avoid 'tinieblas' which is Castilian. 'Achador' (finder) is a Galician nominalization — verify it sounds natural rather than forced.

items.candidate:
"O camiño do home xusto
está cercado por todas partes
polas iniquidades dos egoístas
e pola tiranía dos homes malos.
Bendito sexa aquel que,
en nome da caridade e da boa vontade,
pastorea os febles
polo val da escuridade,
pois el é verdadeiramente o gardián do seu irmán
e o atopador de nenos perdidos.
E eu descargarei sobre ti
gran vinganza e furiosa ira
sobre aqueles que tenten envelenar
e destruír os meus irmáns.
E sabedes que o meu nome é o Señor
cando descargue a miña vinganza

issues.justification: The candidate drops the final clause 'when I lay my vengeance upon thee,' so the iconic concluding threat and cadence are incomplete.
It shifts the biblical, archaic address from the required plural 'vós' to singular 'ti' in several places, weakening the sacred, sermon-like register emphasized in the analysis.
Some phrasing is awkward in Galician, especially 'o atopador de nenos perdidos' and 'cercado por todas partes,' which sound less natural than the reference's diction.

items.source: English, motherfucker! Do you speak it?

general.text: Said at gunpoint to Brett, who has just failed to answer Jules's question clearly. The line derives its power from the collision of the mundane (demanding someone speak a common language) with extreme profanity and mortal threat. Jules's rhetorical style — using language as a weapon — is central to his character. The line has become one of the most quoted in cinema precisely because of this compression: maximum menace in seven words.
general.nb: The profanity must be preserved at full strength. Euphemisms destroy the effect. Both Spanish and Galician have direct equivalents for 'motherfucker' that carry comparable crudeness.

glg.nb: 'Fillo de puta' is the correct Galician form (not 'hijo de puta'). The verb 'fálalo' (do you speak it) uses the Galician clitic pronoun construction; the Castilian-influenced 'lo hablas' should be avoided.

issues.justification: The candidate omits 'speak it' in a full, clear imperative form and reduces the line to an incomplete fragment, weakening the force of the original threat.
It uses a different punctuation and rhythm than the quoted, explosive delivery; the exclamation plus question mark combination is less punchy than the reference's compact insult.

items.reference: Zed está morto, cariño. Zed está morto.

general.text: Butch says this to Fabienne after killing Zed and escaping the pawn shop. The line is a masterpiece of compression: 'baby' (addressing Fabienne tenderly) placed inside a sentence about violent death creates a tonal collision that defines Butch's character — someone capable of extreme violence who is also genuinely tender with his partner. The repetition is not redundant; it has a rhythmic, almost elegiac quality, as if Butch is processing what just happened by saying it twice.
general.nb: 'Baby' as a term of endearment does not translate literally. 'Cariño' (darling/sweetheart) is the natural Iberian Spanish equivalent. The key constraint is that the word must be short and warm — a long endearment would break the rhythm.

glg.nb: 'Morto' is the correct Galician adjective (not 'muerto'). 'Cariño' is also used in Galician, making this one of the easier items to translate into the language.

items.candidate:
Zed está morto, nena.
Zed está morto.

items.source: I'm Winston Wolf. I solve problems.

general.text: Harvey Keitel's introduction of his character. Two sentences, eight words: a complete portrait of a man who is efficient, self-assured, and utterly unimpressed by the chaos he has been called to manage. 'I solve problems' is a masterpiece of understatement — his 'problem' is two dead bodies and a blood-soaked car. The translator's only task is to resist the temptation to add anything. The brevity IS the meaning.
general.nb: Do not add any softening words or expansions. The Spanish and Galician versions should be equally terse.

glg.nb: 'Son' (I am) and 'resolvo' (I solve) are correct Galician first-person present forms. This item is straightforward — the challenge is purely stylistic: preserving the clipped, businesslike rhythm.

items.candidate: Son Winston Wolf. Eu resolvo problemas.

issues.justification: 'Eu resolvo problemas' is correct, but the added explicit subject 'Eu' makes the introduction slightly less clipped and self-assured than the terse reference.

items.source: This is some serious gourmet shit. Me and Vincent would've been satisfied with some freeze-dried Taster's Choice, right? And he springs this serious gourmet shit on us.
items.reference: Isto é unha merda gourmet de verdade. A min e a Vincent chegábanos cun Taster's Choice liofilizado, ¿non si? E o tío vai e réganmos con esta merda gourmet de primeira.

general.text: Jules, at Jimmie's house in the morning after a crisis, evaluates the coffee with the vocabulary of a food critic. 'Serious gourmet shit' is a deliberate contradiction in register — 'gourmet' (elevated, Latinate) and 'shit' (crude, monosyllabic) in a single noun phrase. The contrast with 'freeze-dried Taster's Choice' (a cheap instant coffee brand) sharpens the joke. Jules applies the same intense philosophical attention to a cup of coffee that he applies to scripture and murder.
general.nb: The crude word must remain — replacing 'shit' with a softer synonym eliminates the register clash. 'Taster's Choice' is a brand unknown in Spain/Galicia; a note or substitution with a locally recognizable cheap instant coffee may be appropriate, though keeping the brand name also conveys its foreignness.

glg.nb: 'Merda' is the correct Galician equivalent of 'mierda'. 'Chegábanos' (it was enough for us) uses the Galician imperfect with dative clitic. 'Réganmos' (he douses/treats us to) is a colloquial choice — verify it is natural to a Galician speaker; an alternative is 'e el vai e sácanos esta merda gourmet'.

items.candidate:
Isto é unha merda gourmet seria.
Eu e Vincent teriamos quedado satisfeitos
cun Taster's Choice
liofilizado, non?
E el sácanos
esta merda gourmet seria.

issues.justification: 'Seria' is a slightly less natural modifier after 'merda gourmet' than the reference's more idiomatic phrasing, and it softens the punch of the register clash a bit.
'Eu e Vincent teriamos quedado satisfeitos' is understandable, but the conditional construction feels somewhat heavier and less conversational than the reference's flow.

items.source: Hamburgers! The cornerstone of any nutritious breakfast.
items.reference: ¡As hamburguesas! A pedra angular de calquera almorzo nutritivo.

general.text: Jules says this at the diner, eating breakfast with the philosophical calm of someone who has just committed violence. 'The cornerstone of any nutritious breakfast' is a parody of 1980s American cereal commercials and public health messaging, applied absurdly to hamburgers. The irony is twofold: hamburgers are not breakfast food, and Jules — a hitman — has no business citing nutritional guidelines. The mock-earnest infomercial register is everything; it must not be played as sarcasm.
general.nb: 'Cornerstone' (pedra angular / piedra angular) is the idiomatic equivalent in both languages. 'Almorzo' is the correct Galician word for breakfast; avoid 'desayuno' which is Castilian.

glg.nb: 'Almorzo' (breakfast) is one of the clearest markers of Galician vs. Castilian. Using 'desayuno' here would be a significant error for a Galician speaker.

items.candidate:
Hamburguesas!
A pedra angular
de calquera almorzo nutritivo.

summary.interpretation: 3.00 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.60 major issues per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5)
